title: Regionally partitioned usage mirroring and data governance
preview:
Summary: Implement regionally-partitioned mirroring for Console usage analytics so tenant data can be kept within declared data residency regions, while preserving queryability for cross-region operational dashboards. This task covers pipeline design, access controls, key management, and a tested migration/backfill path.

Goals: 
- Each tenant can declare a residency policy (primary region, optional mirrors) enforced through the ingest and storage layers.
- Short-term: start with EU/US/APAC triage regions and provide per-tenant isolation; long-term: support arbitrary region sets.
- Allow Console product and ops dashboards to surface aggregated metrics while preventing raw tenant-level reads outside allowed regions.

Acceptance criteria: 
- Ingest path attaches region metadata and routes to region-partitioned Kafka topics and regional S3 buckets (or object store) based on tenant residency.
- Asynchronous mirror pipeline copies approved subsets to mirror regions for tenants who opt in; mirror operations are auditable with per-tenant KMS key usage.
- Query service layer enforces residency: queries that would return tenant-level raw events from disallowed regions are denied or redacted.
- Backfill utility can move historical events into new regional buckets with integrity checks and a documented dry-run mode.
- Cost/egress estimator appears in Console settings for tenants to understand mirror costs.

Proposed design (high level):
1) Tenant residency model: extend tenant profile with residency: {primary_region, allowed_mirrors, legal_tag}. Residency is authoritative for write routing.
2) Ingest: Console backend writes events to a tenant-scoped Kafka topic namespace: analytics.<region>.<tenant_shard>. Producers stamp events with tenant_id and residency_header. A small routing service (idempotent) fans out to regional topics if mirrors enabled.
3) Regional storage: Each region has its own object store path and partitioned Parquet ingestion job. Data encrypted with region KMS key; mirror copies must be re-encrypted with mirror region KMS keys.
4) Query enforcement: Metadata service maps tenant->allowed regions. Query proxies check the region attribute on events and deny raw-level reads unless caller is authorized and within allowed region list. Aggregations across tenants remain available as aggregated-only queries.
5) Backfill & repair: Backfill job runs in tenant's primary region, validates checksums, and writes to target regional buckets. Provide a dry-run stateful plan that emits estimated egress and time.

Trade-offs & rationale:
- Strong per-tenant isolation increases storage duplication and egress; chosen approach uses async mirror to avoid blocking writes and to keep ingestion latency low.
- Re-encryption at mirror time simplifies KMS boundary requirements but adds CPU cost; alternative (shared decryptable blobs) rejected for compliance reasons.
- Query-level enforcement keeps operational dashboards usable while preventing leakage; downside is additional query latency for enforcement checks (mitigated by caching residency lookups).

Operational considerations:
- Monitoring: per-region lag metrics (Kafka consumer lag, mirror lag), per-tenant mirror error rates, egress costs, and KMS failures must be added to the Console analytics dashboard.
- SLOs: Ingest write path latency <200ms (no mirrors), mirror completion <5 minutes for 95% of events for normal steady-state, backfill throughput throttled to avoid impacting primary region.
- Runbooks: fail-open vs fail-close policies for mirror failures documented; default is fail-open for writes (so customers' ingestion is not blocked) with alerting and auto-retry.

Migration plan: 
Phase 1: Tenant opt-in pilot with 5 internal tenants in EU and US. Validate mirroring, encryption, query enforcement.
Phase 2: Bulk migration tooling for customers that request residency changes, provide customer-facing timelines and egress/price estimates.
Phase 3: Default enforcement turned on for new tenants in regulated jurisdictions.

Testing & validation: 
- Unit and integration tests for routing and re-encryption.
- End-to-end canary that writes synthetic events and asserts region-local visibility, mirrored visibility, and aggregated dashboard correctness.
- Pen test of residency enforcement logic and KMS access patterns.

Risks: higher egress and storage costs, increased operational complexity, subtle bugs in query enforcement that could leak data. Mitigation includes strong audits, independent security review, and phased rollout.

Artifacts: Confluence design: https://confluence.redwood.ai/display/ENG/Regional+Analytics+Mirroring, RFC PR: https://github.com/redwood-inference/rfcs/pull/84, Draft infra helm charts: https://github.com/redwood-inference/infra/tree/eng/analytics-region-mirror, cost estimator prototype: https://dashboards.redwood.ai/d/abcd-usage/region-cost-estimates

4) Query enforcement: Metadata service maps tenant->allowed regions. Query proxies check the region attribute on events and deny raw-level reads unless caller is authorized and within allowed region list. Aggregations across tenants remain available as aggregated-only queries.
5) Backfill & repair: Backfill job runs in tenant's primary region, validates checksums, and writes to target regional buckets. Provide a dry-run stateful plan that emits estimated egress and time.

Artifacts: Confluence design: https://confluence.redwood.ai/display/ENG/Regional+Analytics+Mirroring, RFC PR: https://github.com/redwood-inference/rfcs/pull/84, Draft infra helm charts: https://github.com/redwood-inference/infra/tree/eng/analytics-region-mirror, cost estimator prototype: https://dashboards.redwood.ai/d/abcd-usage/region-cost-estimates

title: Allocation throttler and reuse-hinting API for multi-tenant paged-attention
preview:
Problem: multi-tenant workloads with bursty long-context requests cause disproportionate KV residency pressure and spike decode tail latency for other tenants. Existing LRU-ish reclamation reacts too late and kernel candidacy selection suffers when prefills are evicted mid-decode.

Goal: implement a lightweight allocation throttler that enforces soft per-tenant allocation budgets for paged KV while exposing a reuse-hinting API so higher-level schedulers (routing, tenant-weighting, canary controllers) can mark fragments/pages as high-reuse or ephemeral. This should reduce cross-tenant thrashing, keep decode tail SLOs stable, and improve kernel selection decisions by providing residency stability signals.

Scope: design and implement runtime-side throttler + public/internal reuse-hinting API + admission rules in the prefill pipeline. Instrument and measure impact on tail latency, eviction rate, kernel fallback frequency, and cost per token. Provide a canary rollout tied to runtime-1.20.

Design notes:
- Throttler: token-bucket like soft quotas per-tenant per-shard measured in "reservation credits" consumed on allocation of new fragments/pages. Credits replenish slowly based on configured tenant weight and observed hit-rate. Overcommit allowed but penalized by lower reuse priority.
- Reuse-hinting API: runtime call reuse_hint(fragment_id, priority_hint) where priority_hint = {high, normal, ephemeral}. Hints are advisory; allocator uses them to bias retention and prefill ordering. Expose SDK-level helper for Dedicated/Private agents to annotate requests by workload.
- Interaction with kernel selection: scheduler will consider retention probability score when choosing kernel variant for prefill-heavy paths so heavy-prefill kernels avoid creating many small fragments that would be evicted immediately.
- Fallbacks: when throttled, requests fall back to compressed-quantized kernel path or shorter prefill window. Provide configurable policies per deployment mode (hosted vs dedicated vs private).

Acceptance criteria:
- End-to-end change gated behind runtime flag feature.paged.kv.throttler and reused-hinting.v1.
- 95th/99th percentile decode latency for synthetic mixed-tenant benchmark decreases by >=10% relative to baseline with same throughput.
- Eviction churn (fragment allocate->evict within 5s) reduced by >=20% on the benchmark.
- No correctness regressions measured via existing prompt fidelity test harness.

Rollout plan: dev feature flag -> internal canary on dedicated pool -> staged rollout to hosted non-production -> hosted production with throttling conservative defaults -> tune based on telemetry.

Longer-term follow-ups: authoritative cross-tenant allocator that maintains soft fairness, and a declarative tenant QoS API for customers to set weights and burst budgets.

title: Preflight policy adapter for VPC Terraform pipelines — OPA/Conftest integration and live migration checks
preview: Add a reusable 'policy adapter' step to Redwood's VPC Terraform CI/CD pipelines that runs OPA/Conftest checks against terraform plan output and performs migration safety analysis for module upgrades and stateful changes. The adapter should: (1) consume a standardized terraform plan JSON or tfplan, (2) execute curated Conftest/OPA policies (security, networking, cost, and data-plane impact), (3) surface human-friendly failure reasons with remediation hints, and (4) provide an extensible scoring/allowlist mechanism so on-call and platform engineers can tune gating sensitivity. Additionally the adapter will run a migration health analyzer to detect high-risk changes to stateful resources (subnet CIDR changes, route table swaps, RDS/network endpoints) and surface rollout strategies (canary apply, emergency rollback playbook) as pipeline artifacts.
text: Add a reusable 'policy adapter' step to Redwood's VPC Terraform CI/CD pipelines that runs OPA/Conftest checks against terraform plan output and performs migration safety analysis for module upgrades and stateful changes. The adapter should: (1) consume a standardized terraform plan JSON or tfplan, (2) execute curated Conftest/OPA policies (security, networking, cost, and data-plane impact), (3) surface human-friendly failure reasons with remediation hints, and (4) provide an extensible scoring/allowlist mechanism so on-call and platform engineers can tune gating sensitivity. Additionally the adapter will run a migration health analyzer to detect high-risk changes to stateful resources (subnet CIDR changes, route table swaps, RDS/network endpoints) and surface rollout strategies (canary apply, emergency rollback playbook) as pipeline artifacts.

title: release-bridge: automated TTL pin rotation and signed-profile reissue
preview:
Context:
Redwood needs a safe, automated path to rotate model version pins and republish performance profiles when we onboard new artifacts. Today engineers manually pin builds and push profiles which causes drift and delayed promotions. This ticket builds an automation layer ("release-bridge") that enforces TTL-based pin rotation, signs artifacts and profiles, and atomically promotes signed profile bundles into the catalog with a staged canary.

Goals:
- Implement TTL-based pin lifecycle: pins created by CI expire after configurable TTL unless explicitly extended by a human-approved hold.
- Ensure every published model artifact and associated performance profile is cryptographically signed and has provenance metadata (build id, git commit, profile hash, kernel tag).
- Automate reissuance of signed profiles when a pin rotates (e.g., rebuild profile manifest with new pin and re-sign).
- Integrate a staged promotion flow: canary -> phased -> global, gated by perf/latency/regression checks recorded in the console.
- Provide clear audit trail and rollback for any automated promotion (searchable in console + linkable Git revs).

Design summary:
- release-bridge service (new) orchestrates GitHub Actions jobs: build -> test -> sign -> publish. It stores ephemeral pin leases in the catalog datastore with TTL metadata.
- Signing uses cosign + KMS-backed keys stored in our HSM integration. Cosign signatures and SBOM-like provenance JSON are uploaded alongside the model artifact and profile.
- A lightweight policy engine evaluates metrics from the staging runner (latency P50, P95, tail errors, and quality checks from eval harness). If thresholds pass, release-bridge advances the profile to next promotion step and extends the pin lease as needed.
- TTL default set to 72h to avoid pin stagnation; human override via console or CLI with audit record required.

Acceptance criteria:
1) CI pipeline job 'release-bridge/publish' signs artifacts and uploads signature + provenance to artifact store for a sample model (open-llama-7b test).
2) Pin entries created by the job appear in catalog with TTL and owner metadata. Expired pins are auto-unpinned by the service and cause dependent routes to fallback to last-stable.
3) Canary promotion runs three sample traffic slices and gating checks; pass -> phased promotion triggered automatically; fail -> automated rollback and alert to #onboarding-alerts.
4) All artifacts and profiles have verifiable signatures (cosign verify) and provenance JSON includes build_id, commit_sha, profile_version, kernel_tag.
5) Console exposes pin history and signature links for the promoted profile.

Out of scope for this ticket:
- Full UI work for mass pin management (will follow in PM ticket).
- Multi-region promotion orchestration (next phase).

title: Adaptive VPC sensing: unified probes for latency, errors, saturation, and token flow
preview:
Problem: Private VPC deployments currently lack a unified, configurable probe surface for signaling latency degradation, rising error rates, instance saturation, and token flow constraints. Existing checks are fragmented: some services export latency percentiles, others expose GPU metrics; there's no canonical token throughput signal or saturation arbitration that can be consumed by the control plane and Terraform IaC modules. This causes noisy alerts, inconsistent autoscaling behavior in Dedicated/Private, and slow incident response for customer VPCs.

Goals:
- Define a minimal but expressive set of probes that run in-customer VPC and export to Redwood's agent/collector (prometheus remote_write compatible) while preserving minimal egress.
- Cover four probe domains: latency (p50/p95/p99), error rates (5xx and request-level failures), saturation (queue/backlog, GPU/CPU utilization, kv-cache pressure), and token flow (tokens/sec, tokens/backlog, per-model limits).
- Make probe configuration declarative and pluggable via the existing Terraform modules so customers can opt-in, tune thresholds, and wire alerts into their on-prem/CloudWatch/PagerDuty stacks.
- Provide clear rollback/upgrade story for the agent and probe schema to avoid breaking older Private control planes.

Non-goals:
- Replacing all customer-side observability (we will rely on exporting canonical metrics, not forcing a specific dashboard tool).
- Implementing ML-model-level quality evals here (out of scope).

Design summary:
1) Probe contract: each probe is a time-series with a stable metric name prefix: redwood.vpc.probe.<domain> (e.g. redwood.vpc.probe.latency_ms). Labels: instance_id, model_family, region, probe_version. Values are numeric (ms, count, tokens/sec). Probe version is used for safe schema upgrades.

2) Latency: instrument HTTP/gRPC frontends and runtime: emit histogram buckets and compute p50/p95/p99 in prometheus or at the collector. Default SLOs: p95 < 120ms (interactive routes), p99 < 350ms. For batch-heavy routes, separate profile.

3) Error rates: emit counters for request_total and request_error (5xx and system_errors). Derive error_rate over 5m and 1h windows. Alerting thresholds: 5m error_rate > 1% (warning), > 5% (critical).

4) Saturation: emit queue_length, pending_kv_entries, gpu_util_pct, cpu_util_pct. Build a synthetic saturation index (0-100) as a weighted combination (configurable). If index > 75 for 2 consecutive minutes, mark instance as saturated for routing/fallback.

5) Token flow: new metric token_emitted_per_sec and token_backlog (tokens waiting for generation). Rate limits and soft-cap signals: if token_emitted_per_sec drops >20% vs baseline while backlog grows, consider serving fallback model or shedding lower-priority requests. SLOs measured per-model-family and per-customer.

Instrumentation & IaC:
- Terraform: add module outputs and variables in modules/private-agent to expose probe config (probe_version, scrape_interval, allowed_labels, egress_endpoint). Default scrape_interval = 15s.
- The module will support two modes: push (agent pushes aggregates to Redwood collector over TLS) and scrape (Redwood-managed scraper inside customer VPC with a ServiceAccount). For air-gapped installs, push-only with optional local metrics bundle.
- Module will create IAM roles and security group rules as opt-in resources; by default no public endpoints are opened.

Rollout & backward compatibility:
- Version probes with probe_version label. Collector ignores unknown probe_version by default and logs for 30d. Migration plan: 1) deploy agent to 10% of clusters with probe_version=v2, 2) validate metrics and dashboards, 3) flip default in module, 4) deprecate v1 after 90d.

Operations:
- Dashboards: template dashboards (latency/error/saturation/token flow) shipped as JSON in infra repo and link provided below.
- Alerts: map warning/critical to automated remediation: warning -> create ticket + fasttrace; critical -> trigger canary fallback (route traffic to hosted/dedicated pool) and oncall page.

Acceptance criteria:
- Terraform module change merged and published (module version bumped).
- Agent emits all required metrics in internal staging and at least 3 regression tests for schema compatibility.
- Dashboards render p50/p95/p99 correctly and alerting rules produce expected signals in synthetic load test.
- Upgrade path documented and smoke-tested across private clusters.

Artifacts:
- Design doc: https://confluence.redwood.ai/pages/PRIVATE-PROBE-DESIGN (internal)
- Infra PR: https://github.com/redwood-inference/infra/pull/1849 (WIP)
- Agent PR: https://github.com/redwood-inference/agent/pull/422 (draft)
- Dashboard JSON: https://drive.redwood.ai/d/1-probe-dashboard-vpc.json

Open questions: (1) Do we compute p99 at the agent or at collector to reduce cardinality? (2) How to express multi-tenant token baselines safely without leaking cross-customer aggregates? (3) Should saturation index be standardized or left as customer-defined expression?

Next steps: finish infra PR, add unit tests for token metrics aggregation, schedule a review with security for egress model, and run a 10% rollout to staging clusters.

title: Long-lived stream stalls from proxy/TLS lifecycle — runtime guard, SDK guidance, and customer bridge
preview: Multiple customers reported high rates of 5xx/timeout errors during long-lived streaming sessions (SSE / chunked responses). Initial triage found correlation with prolonged TLS session lifecycles and intermittent proxy-side TLS renegotiation on the edge path. When the renegotiation window overlapped with a client-initiated keepalive gap, connections would stall, the frontdoor would reset and cause upstream retries to cascade into tenant-visible 5xx spikes. This ticket tracks the targeted runtime guard, SDK guidance, short-term customer bridge and a safe rollout to prevent recurrence.
text: Multiple customers reported high rates of 5xx/timeout errors during long-lived streaming sessions (SSE / chunked responses). Initial triage found correlation with prolonged TLS session lifecycles and intermittent proxy-side TLS renegotiation on the edge path. When the renegotiation window overlapped with a client-initiated keepalive gap, connections would stall, the frontdoor would reset and cause upstream retries to cascade into tenant-visible 5xx spikes. This ticket tracks the targeted runtime guard, SDK guidance, short-term customer bridge and a safe rollout to prevent recurrence.

title: Route Sentry: route-level observability & alerting for embedding+ranker endpoints
preview:
Goal: Provide route-scoped dashboards, tracing enrichment, token-level cost breakdowns, and automated alerts for embedding + reranking endpoints so product owners can detect quality regressions, latency spikes, and cost anomalies tied to specific routes and model versions.

Scope: instrument runtime and scorer layers to emit route_id, request_tags (caller_id, route_name), embedding_model_version, rerank_model_version, candidate_count, kv_cache_hit, batch_size, vector_index_latency_ms, scorer_latency_ms, tokens_generated, token_cost_usd, compute_gpu_ms. Build dashboards per-route (p50/p95 latency, SLI: successful responses, rerank_precision@10, embedding_distance_drift, cost_per_request, cost_per_1k_tokens) and a cross-route heatmap for sudden delta detection. Implement alert rules for: 1) p95 latency increase > 30% for 15m; 2) cost per request increase > 40% over baseline for 1h; 3) rerank_precision@10 drop > 10% on evaluated prompt set; 4) sudden rise in kv_cache_miss rate > 20%.

Acceptance criteria:
- Traces include route_id and model hashes in 100% of scorer spans.
- Per-route dashboard template available in Console and linked to trace explorer.
- Token-level cost decomposition stored as timeseries and exposed in per-route cost tab.
- Three alert policies wired to PagerDuty and Slack channel #sentry-emb-rank.
- Runbook with diagnostic steps and rollbacks checked into Confluence.

Trade-offs & decisions:
- Sampling: keep 1% full traces for 90 days and store aggregated statistics for all requests. Decision balances disk/TCO vs post-facto debugging needs.
- Granularity: we will not store per-request embeddings for long-term; instead persist summary metrics (mean distance, variance) per route to avoid storage blowup.
- Cost attribution: attribute request cost as embedding_cost + rerank_cost + index_lookup_cost; initial token cost uses dk-token-rate with per-model coefficients until runtime metering is accurate.

Risk & mitigations:
- Increased telemetry volume => throttle spans at client library and rely on aggregated metrics pipeline.
- Alert noise => use warm-up windows, require 3 consecutive evaluation windows before PD.

Open questions:
- Should drift alerts be tied to canary vs production model versions? (Proposed: yes — tag by rollout and include canary flag in spans.)
- Long tail routes with low traffic: what baseline should we use? (Proposed: cohort baseline grouping by request size and candidate_count.)

title: Instrumented profile generator with dataset & prompt registry for onboarding
preview: Problem: Model onboarding currently produces performance profiles in an ad-hoc way: datasets and prompt sets are copied into per-run buckets and eval runners are special-cased. This makes profiles hard to reproduce, pin, and surface in the model catalog. Scope: implement a repeatable, instrumented profile generation flow that centralizes dataset + prompt versioning, provides an immutable profile artifact with provenance, and integrates with the model catalog UI for publishing and pinning. Deliverables: (1) lightweight Dataset + Prompt Registry (metadata only) with versioned identifiers and aliases; (2) instrumented profile generator job (CI/runner) that uses registry entries to run evals across configured eval runners and emits a single profile artifact JSON with metadata (dataset_id, prompt_set_id, model_version, runner_version, hardware_tags, kernel_profile, token-distribution metrics); (3) publish pipeline to upload artifacts to S3 and register the profile with the model catalog (including checksums and schema version); (4) minimal console integration to show published profiles and allow pinning to a model variant.

title: resilience-fuzz-suite-for-partial-streams-and-schema-relaxation-tests
preview: Build a focused test harness that simulates partial/garbled streaming output, constrained-decoder backoff behavior, and schema-relaxation fallbacks across model variants and quantization profiles. Goal is to catch regressions where streaming + function/tool calling or token-level truncation leads to invalid JSON payloads or silent schema drift. The suite will be integrated into CI and run nightly against a matrix of runtime kernels and model variants.
text: Build a focused test harness that simulates partial/garbled streaming output, constrained-decoder backoff behavior, and schema-relaxation fallbacks across model variants and quantization profiles. Goal is to catch regressions where streaming + function/tool calling or token-level truncation leads to invalid JSON payloads or silent schema drift. The suite will be integrated into CI and run nightly against a matrix of runtime kernels and model variants.

title: API Launchpad: Model Selection Flow and Code Composer Screenshots
preview: Create a compact, developer-focused asset pack for the API Launchpad (hosted API) flow: model selection, request composer, and multi-language code snippets. These assets will be used in docs, product first-run modal, and the console onboarding card. Focus on: clear model metadata (performance, cost, region), an inline request builder with example payloads, and code snippet toggles for curl/python/js that populate from the request composer. Deliver hi-res PNGs and vector exports, plus a Figma page with annotated layout specs and responsive variants.

title: Robust HTTP Event Reassembly and Backoff Protocol for Streaming Responses
preview: Problem: Clients intermittently lose SSE connections during long-running responses (tool calls, structured JSON partials). Current reconnect behavior sometimes produces duplicate function-call side effects, garbled partial JSON, or stalls when the server switches model backends. Goal: Define and implement a robust HTTP event reassembly protocol and client-server backoff strategy that provides: idempotent resume semantics, clear chunk boundaries for structured outputs and function-call transcripts, graceful partial-output rehydration, and a compatibility header that enables gradual rollout across hosted/dedicated/private deployments.
text: Problem: Clients intermittently lose SSE connections during long-running responses (tool calls, structured JSON partials). Current reconnect behavior sometimes produces duplicate function-call side effects, garbled partial JSON, or stalls when the server switches model backends. Goal: Define and implement a robust HTTP event reassembly protocol and client-server backoff strategy that provides: idempotent resume semantics, clear chunk boundaries for structured outputs and function-call transcripts, graceful partial-output rehydration, and a compatibility header that enables gradual rollout across hosted/dedicated/private deployments.

title: Shard-based usage republish protocol for VPC/offline collectors
preview:
Problem: In VPC/private deployments we regularly see windows where the control-plane cannot accept live telemetry (network maintenance, customer firewall rules, or intentional air-gapped export). During these windows on-prem collectors buffer and later attempt to ship usage. Existing ad-hoc bundling leads to two recurring issues: 1) missing events when a collector fails to replay a partial bundle, and 2) duplicate events when retries are not deduplicated by the control plane. Both cause billing inaccuracies and customer trust risk.

Proposed solution: implement a shard-based republish protocol for on-prem/VPC collectors that combines compact immutable snapshots + append-only delta windows. Each collector partitions usage into logical shards (tenant-day or tenant-range) and assigns monotonically increasing segment IDs. Clients ship two artifact types to the control plane over a secure channel or upload manifest: (A) snapshot (compact, signed summary of state with segment sequence numbers and a small Merkle-of-batches hash) and (B) delta segments (append-only lists of events with batch_id and segment_seq). The control plane will accept at-least-once deliveries but enforce idempotency using (tenant_id, shard_id, segment_seq) and a deterministic batch fingerprint. A small local dedup store on the collector (LRU cache keyed by batch fingerprint) plus segment tombstones enable safe GC.

Key design constraints: keep bandwidth low for air-gapped USB manifests; surfacing small human-readable manifests is required for compliance audits; ensure reconcilers can deterministically compute final counts from the sequence; tolerate out-of-order arrival; avoid global cross-tenant locks.

Success criteria: end-to-end accounting delta < 0.5% across synthetic workload tests and < 24h median reconciliation time after extended offline window; zero unreconciled segments older than 7 days in staging; per-tenant duplicate ratio < 0.01% in long-run tests.

Open questions: shard sizing for heavy multi-tenant hosts, retention/GC policy for dedup state, whether to require manifest signing via customer KMS vs. platform signing, and backwards compatibility with old collector bundles.

The control plane will accept at-least-once deliveries but enforce idempotency using (tenant_id, shard_id, segment_seq) and a deterministic batch fingerprint. title: SLO cohort preemptor with GPU fragmentation reduction
preview:
Problem statement: Continuous batching currently favors throughput but can cause unfair latency regressions for bursty tenants and creates GPU memory fragmentation from repeated small KV cache evictions. We need an SLO-aware preemption policy that groups tenants into latency cohorts and performs preemptive slot retirement + opportunistic compaction to reduce fragmentation while preserving throughput and overall GPU utilization.

Goals:
- Keep tenant P95/P99 latency within configured SLO bands during normal and bursty loads.
- Improve memory fragmentation (free slab count and largest contiguous free region) to reduce out-of-memory spikes for large requests.
- Maintain end-to-end throughput within 5% of baseline for steady-state workloads.
- Provide observability and safe rollout via feature flags and canaries.

Non-goals: full KV GC redesign, global tenant rebalancer, or changing model selection routing decisions in this ticket.
text:

We need an SLO-aware preemption policy that groups tenants into latency cohorts and performs preemptive slot retirement + opportunistic compaction to reduce fragmentation while preserving throughput and overall GPU utilization.

Goals:
- Keep tenant P95/P99 latency within configured SLO bands during normal and bursty loads.
- Improve memory fragmentation (free slab count and largest contiguous free region) to reduce out-of-memory spikes for large requests.
- Maintain end-to-end throughput within 5% of baseline for steady-state workloads.
- Provide observability and safe rollout via feature flags and canaries.

Non-goals: full KV GC redesign, global tenant rebalancer, or changing model selection routing decisions in this ticket.

title: EKS control plane upgrade orchestrator + safety hooks for Private
preview:
Problem: Our current EKS provisioning module for Redwood Private lacks an orchestrated, idempotent upgrade path that combines safe pre-upgrade assertions, live health probes, and an automated rollback window. Customers in regulated environments need predictable, auditable upgrades that do not rely on manual canaries or ad-hoc scripts.

Goal: Implement a Terraform-friendly 'upgrade orchestrator' layer inside the existing control-plane module that: (1) exposes safe upgrade toggles and lifecyle hooks, (2) wires in post-upgrade health probes and quantitative SLO checks, (3) records upgrade metadata for audits, and (4) supports an automated rollback window if metrics degrade. The module must be usable in both self-managed Private installs and Redwood-managed Dedicated clusters.

Scope / Acceptance Criteria:
- Add new Terraform resources/locals/outputs that allow invoking an upgrade orchestration workflow (flag: enable_upgrade_orchestrator = true).
- Provide lifecycle hooks: pre_upgrade (scripts/commands), canary_promote (optional), post_upgrade_check (HTTP/K8s readiness + custom metric assertions).
- Implement a lightweight stateful 'upgrade-runner' (implemented as a Kubernetes Job + ConfigMap managed by the module) that performs the orchestration steps; job should be optionally disabled to support air-gapped installs.
- Health checks must include: API health (control plane endpoints), node readiness, KV cache integrity probe (application-level), and a custom latency/5xx rate threshold against a synthetic test endpoint. Thresholds must be configurable via module inputs.
- The module must export upgrade metadata (timestamp, user, upstream-k8s-version, previous-manifest-hash, outcome) to a provided S3/GCS/Audit sink or to an optional internal Audit CRD.
- Rollback: if post_upgrade checks fail within 'rollback_window_minutes' (default 30), orchestration should attempt automated rollback to previous manifests and mark the upgrade as failed.
- Provide comprehensive docs: usage example, migration notes, required IAM permissions, and recommended observability dashboards.
- Add unit and integration test manifests for CI that run the orchestrator against a lightweight EKS cluster (kind or eks-fargate wrapper) and simulate a failing post-upgrade check.

Design Considerations / Tradeoffs:
- We avoid embedding heavy controller logic in TF; the module will deploy a small k8s Job/Runner that performs orchestration. This preserves declarative nature while allowing procedural steps.
- For air-gapped installs we must support delivering the runner image via private registry and allow disabling S3/GCS audit exports.
- We will provide both a 'canaryless' fast path (no external canary service) and an optional canary promotion flow for customers who want staged traffic shifting.

Implementation Tasks (high level):
- Terraform: add new inputs/outputs, create k8s manifest templates for Job, ConfigMap, ServiceAccount, Role/RoleBinding. Ensure IAM for Runner to read/write audit sink.
- Runner: small Go/python binary to execute lifecycle hooks, call K8s API, probe endpoints, publish audit events. Container image build + CV scan.
- Health Probes: reuse existing synthetic probe library used by Console; expose probe config via module variables.
- CI: integration test harness + a GitHub Actions workflow job that runs against an EKS test cluster.
- Docs: examples/private/upgrade-orchestrator.md, runbook entries for manual rollback and incident playbook.

Out-of-scope: full traffic-shifting service mesh integration (will be handled in a follow-up that hooks into Canary Gateway), multi-region cross-cluster orchestrated upgrades (future).

Risk & Mitigations:
- Risk: Runner has permission to perform destructive actions. Mitigation: least-privilege ServiceAccount, detailed IAM matrices in docs, dry-run mode for validation.
- Risk: False positives on health checks trigger unnecessary rollback. Mitigation: allow a grace period and configurable sensitivity, and require two consecutive failures before rollback.

Deliverables:
- Terraform module changes under modules/eks-controlplane/upgrade-orchestrator
- Container image and source for runner under infra/upgrade-runner
- CI integration tests and a canary failure test case
- Docs and runbook PRs

Acceptance tests (manual):
- Run module with enable_upgrade_orchestrator=true against a dev Private cluster, trigger a k8s version bump, verify: runner executes pre/post hooks, post checks pass, metadata exported, no rollback.
- Repeat but inject a failing post-check and verify automated rollback and audit entry.

Notes / Links:
- Confluence design draft: https://confluence.redwood.ai/display/INF/eks-upgrade-orchestrator (placeholder)
- Prototype PR: https://github.com/redwood-inference/infra/pull/987 (placeholder)
- Observability dashboard: https://grafana.redwood.ai/d/upgrade-orchestrator (placeholder)

title: Incident slate: lightweight incident artifact and escalation attachments
preview:
Background:
Redwood's escalation channels (PagerDuty + Slack) currently post full evidence bundles which are heavyweight and often duplicate information across attachments. This work introduces an \"incident slate\": a compact, structured artifact (JSON blob + small rich attachment) generated at signal ingestion and included in PD and Slack notifications. The slate is designed to: surface the minimal set of decision-making facts, link to the detailed evidence bundle, and seed runbook templates and postmortem drafts automatically.

Goals:
- Reduce cognitive load for oncall by surfacing the top 3 signals (latency spike, error class, top affected customers) and immediate remediation hints.
- Ensure the slate can be consumed programmatically by runbook automation and postmortem seeding systems.
- Preserve privacy: redact PII and adhere to retention policies before any external notification.
- Make the slate resilient to rate-limited backends and graceful in cases of missing telemetry.

Success metrics (first 12 weeks):
- Median time-to-triage reduced by 25% for P1 latency incidents across runtime services.
- Oncall positive feedback score >= 4.0 in internal survey for new PD/Slack attachments.
- Automatic postmortem seed acceptance rate >= 70% (seeded drafts require < 2 edits on average).

Design decisions & trade-offs:
1) Compact vs verbose: We selected a 10-field maximum slate instead of full evidence to keep PD cards scannable. Trade-off: may omit low-signal spans that are useful in rare cases; detailed evidence bundle remains linked.
2) Data retention & redaction: Slate will include hashed identifiers (sha256) for customer IDs and redact raw request payloads. This reduces debugging fidelity but is required for privacy and regulator alignment.
3) Generation point: create slate at the tracing ingestion worker (closest to the signal) to minimize telemetry enrichment latency. This increases worker CPU but reduces notification latency.
4) Fallback behavior: If span extraction fails, attach a short summary: \"Trace metadata unavailable — evidence bundle queued\". Prevents noisy PD alerts caused by incomplete payloads.

Slate schema (summary):
- incident_id, short_summary, severity, top_error_class, top_latency_p95_ms, top_3_spans (span_name + relative_time_ms), affected_customers_hashed, suggested_runbook_id, links {evidence_bundle, grafana_slice, trace_link}, generated_at_iso

Acceptance criteria:
- PD integration: PD webhook receives slate JSON in the \"details\" payload and renders a compact card matching the design spec.
- Slack integration: Slack attachment uses Block Kit to show the short_summary + top_error_class and a button linking to the full evidence bundle.
- Runbook seeding: When suggested_runbook_id is present, runbook seeder creates a draft with the slate fields filled and assigns the draft to the incident owner.
- Telemetry: Emit metrics: slate.generated.count, slate.redactions.count, slate.fallback.count, slate.processing.latency_ms (p95/p99).
- Tests: Unit tests for schema validation, e2e test that simulates a latency spike and verifies slate in PD and Slack.

Implementation plan (high level):
1) Slate generator service (4 pts): implement extractor that composes slate from trace + metrics + alerts. Add config to cap included spans to 3 and perform PII redaction.
2) PD adapter (1 pt): extend PD webhook forwarder to embed slate in notification payload and add JSON schema validation.
3) Slack adapter (1 pt): Block Kit template and link handling + localized fallback for mobile clients.
4) Runbook seeder hook (1 pt): small service to accept slate and instantiate runbook draft.
5) Metrics & dashboards (1 pt): new Grafana panels and Alert rules for slate errors/latency.

Rollout plan:
- Week 1 (canary): Internal infra teams only; PD group alerts routed to a dev PD escalation policy; collect feedback.
- Week 2 (expanded): Add a subset of customer-facing services (search, embeddings) behind feature flag.
- Week 3 (full): Default for all new P1 incidents; old attachments deprecated but available via link.
- Post rollout: Measure triage time and iterate on slate fields based on oncall feedback.

QA & validation:
- e2e harness (github action): synthetic load that triggers a latency SLO breach and asserts slate appears in PD and Slack with correct fields.
- Manual walkthrough checklist for SRE oncall: verify redaction, verify runbook seed creation, sanity-check links.

Risks & mitigations:
- Over-redaction reduces debuggability: keep a privacy-exempt audit mode only accessible to SREs for 7 days.
- Increased generation latency: move heavy enrichment to async background job if 95th percentile exceeds 150ms.
- PD rate limits: throttle notifications and collapse repeated slates into a single update with incrementing summary.

Longer-term extensions:
- Integrate with postmortem auto-population: map slate fields to postmortem sections (impact, timeline seeds).
- Enrich slate with model inference traces for Redwood Dedicated/private customers when available.
- Allow customer opt-in for non-redacted slates when contractually permitted.

- Ensure the slate can be consumed programmatically by runbook automation and postmortem seeding systems.
- Preserve privacy: redact PII and adhere to retention policies before any external notification.
- Make the slate resilient to rate-limited backends and graceful in cases of missing telemetry.

Success metrics (first 12 weeks):

- Oncall positive feedback score >= 4.0 in internal survey for new PD/Slack attachments.
- Automatic postmortem seed acceptance rate >= 70% (seeded drafts require < 2 edits on average).

Design decisions & trade-offs:
1) Compact vs verbose: We selected a 10-field maximum slate instead of full evidence to keep PD cards scannable. Trade-off: may omit low-signal spans that are useful in rare cases; detailed evidence bundle remains linked.
2) Data retention & redaction: Slate will include hashed identifiers (sha256) for customer IDs and redact raw request payloads. This reduces debugging fidelity but is required for privacy and regulator alignment.
3) Generation point: create slate at the tracing ingestion worker (closest to the signal) to minimize telemetry enrichment latency. This increases worker CPU but reduces notification latency.
4) Fallback behavior: If span extraction fails, attach a short summary: \"Trace metadata unavailable — evidence bundle queued\". Prevents noisy PD alerts caused by incomplete payloads.

Slate schema (summary):
- incident_id, short_summary, severity, top_error_class, top_latency_p95_ms, top_3_spans (span_name + relative_time_ms), affected_customers_hashed, suggested_runbook_id, links {evidence_bundle, grafana_slice, trace_link}, generated_at_iso

Acceptance criteria:
- PD integration: PD webhook receives slate JSON in the \"details\" payload and renders a compact card matching the design spec.
- Slack integration: Slack attachment uses Block Kit to show the short_summary + top_error_class and a button linking to the full evidence bundle.
- Runbook seeding: When suggested_runbook_id is present, runbook seeder creates a draft with the slate fields filled and assigns the draft to the incident owner.
- Telemetry: Emit metrics: slate.generated.count, slate.redactions.count, slate.fallback.count, slate.processing.latency_ms (p95/p99).
- Tests: Unit tests for schema validation, e2e test that simulates a latency spike and verifies slate in PD and Slack.

Implementation plan (high level):
1) Slate generator service (4 pts): implement extractor that composes slate from trace + metrics + alerts. Add config to cap included spans to 3 and perform PII redaction.
2) PD adapter (1 pt): extend PD webhook forwarder to embed slate in notification payload and add JSON schema validation.
3) Slack adapter (1 pt): Block Kit template and link handling + localized fallback for mobile clients.
4) Runbook seeder hook (1 pt): small service to accept slate and instantiate runbook draft.
5) Metrics & dashboards (1 pt): new Grafana panels and Alert rules for slate errors/latency.

Rollout plan:
- Week 1 (canary): Internal infra teams only; PD group alerts routed to a dev PD escalation policy; collect feedback.
- Week 2 (expanded): Add a subset of customer-facing services (search, embeddings) behind feature flag.
- Week 3 (full): Default for all new P1 incidents; old attachments deprecated but available via link.
- Post rollout: Measure triage time and iterate on slate fields based on oncall feedback.

QA & validation:
- e2e harness (github action): synthetic load that triggers a latency SLO breach and asserts slate appears in PD and Slack with correct fields.
- Manual walkthrough checklist for SRE oncall: verify redaction, verify runbook seed creation, sanity-check links.

Risks & mitigations:
- Over-redaction reduces debuggability: keep a privacy-exempt audit mode only accessible to SREs for 7 days.
- Increased generation latency: move heavy enrichment to async background job if 95th percentile exceeds 150ms.
- PD rate limits: throttle notifications and collapse repeated slates into a single update with incrementing summary.

Longer-term extensions:
- Integrate with postmortem auto-population: map slate fields to postmortem sections (impact, timeline seeds).
- Enrich slate with model inference traces for Redwood Dedicated/private customers when available.
- Allow customer opt-in for non-redacted slates when contractually permitted.

title: Correlate Pane Sequencer — Request Drilldown & Span Sequencing Prototype
preview: Design a compact, focus+context Correlate Pane that lets engineers go from an observed anomaly in the Console dashboard to a time-aligned, span-sequenced view of a single request session. The pane lives as a slide-over from Dashboards and from Rollouts/Errors rows; it should show: a compact timeline of spans with relative durations, highlight correlation IDs across services, an event ridge/mini-heatmap for async wakes, request metadata (model, route, tokens, cost estimate), quick filters (service, span-kind, status, tag search), and deep-links to full Trace Canvas and Session Replay. Prototype must explore density modes (compact / expanded), keyboard-first navigation, and a contextual actions menu (annotate, pin, start eval, create rollback request). The design must balance information density for quick Triage while keeping path to full investigation obvious.
text: Design a compact, focus+context Correlate Pane that lets engineers go from an observed anomaly in the Console dashboard to a time-aligned, span-sequenced view of a single request session. The pane lives as a slide-over from Dashboards and from Rollouts/Errors rows; it should show: a compact timeline of spans with relative durations, highlight correlation IDs across services, an event ridge/mini-heatmap for async wakes, request metadata (model, route, tokens, cost estimate), quick filters (service, span-kind, status, tag search), and deep-links to full Trace Canvas and Session Replay. Prototype must explore density modes (compact / expanded), keyboard-first navigation, and a contextual actions menu (annotate, pin, start eval, create rollback request). The design must balance information density for quick Triage while keeping path to full investigation obvious.

title: split-stream-aggregator-and-safe-kv-shard-handoff
preview:
Problem:
The current stream aggregator implementation combines control-plane responsibilities (routing, shard ownership, migration coordination) with the fast-path that emits token batches to the GPU. This monolith causes several issues: head-of-line blocking when control updates happen, unpredictable latency spikes during KV shard migrations, and a complex code surface that makes dependency upgrades (tokio/bytes) risky.

Goals:
- Reduce tail latency and jitter on the fast-path by moving all non-essential control logic out of token emission path.
- Introduce a deterministic, lease-based shard handoff protocol that makes KV shard migration safe during rolling upgrades and autoscaling.
- Simplify scheduler internals by replacing a single global mutex with per-shard epoch fencing and translucent priority lanes.
- Complete dependency upgrades (tokio -> 1.29, bytes -> 1.4+) as part of the refactor with minimal risk.

Non-goals:
- Full rewrite of the aggregation algorithms or model scheduling policies. We will keep current batching heuristics unless a clear perf win is identified during perf runs.

High-level approach:
1) Split the monolith into two modules: "stream-coordinator" (control-plane, single-writer per shard, handles ownership, metrics, config updates) and "emit-engine" (hot-path: batch assembly, prefetch wavefront, KV fast reads). The emit-engine will not block on control-plane state transitions; it will observe epoch fences instead.
2) Implement a lease-based handoff protocol: a migrating node obtains a migration lease for a shard, signals wavefront-prefetch to the target, drains outstanding windows under the previous epoch, and then atomically switches the shard epoch. Fallback policy: if handoff stalls > HANDOFF_TIMEOUT, target emits stale-cache fallback and marks metric for operator intervention.
3) Introduce "wavefront prefetch": emit-engine predicts next token prefix windows and warms local KV caches with bounded parallel prefetches. This reduces cold KV latency during long sequences.
4) Scheduler cleanup: replace global admission lock with per-shard epoch counters + per-lane ring buffers. This reduces contention and simplifies reasoning about draining and preemption.
5) Dependency upgrade: run cargo update to tokio 1.29 and bytes 1.4.x; fix API breaks in the control plane and update integration tests.

Success criteria / acceptance tests:
- Median tail latency for 99th percentile pages reduced by at least 30% on synthetic long-sequence workload (baseline: 320ms p99 -> target <= 225ms).
- During a planned rolling migration test with 50 shards, no requests should observe KV inconsistency; any fallback events must be logged and <0.5% of requests.
- CPU and allocation profile shows >15% lower contention time in scheduler mutex traces.
- All unit and integration tests pass; benchmark suite (perf-ci) shows non-regressive p50/p95 for short-sequence low-latency workload.

Rollout plan:
- Phase A: Integration branch implementing split modules behind a feature flag; run soak in staging with simulated traffic.
- Phase B: Enable wavefront prefetch in a single region; monitor tail latency and handoff metrics.
- Phase C: Gradual rollout to all regions with canary on dedicated capacity clusters.

Monitoring and dashboards:
- New metrics: shard_handoff_latency_ms, handoff_failures_total, emit_engine_prefetch_hit_rate, per_shard_epoch, scheduler_lane_contention_ms.
- Perf dashboard link in attachments.

title: Interim usage quarantine and automated tenant-credit simulator for contested enterprise invoices
preview: Summary: Enterprise customers sometimes raise security questionnaire or contract questions that result in a temporary hold/escrow on invoices while usage is investigated. Current operations require manual ledger edits and provisional credits created by Finance, which is slow and error-prone for high-volume metered accounts. This ticket scopes a short-lived product/ops capability: (1) a usage quarantine flow that isolates suspect usage from final invoicing, and (2) an automated tenant-credit simulator (sandbox) that lets CS/Finance/PMs preview prorations, provisional credits, and final invoice outcomes before accepting/committing ledger changes. Goal: reduce mean time-to-resolution for contested usage, lower manual adjustment errors, and provide reproducible experiments for pricing/packaging A/Bs that touch credits/proration logic.
text: Summary: Enterprise customers sometimes raise security questionnaire or contract questions that result in a temporary hold/escrow on invoices while usage is investigated. Current operations require manual ledger edits and provisional credits created by Finance, which is slow and error-prone for high-volume metered accounts. This ticket scopes a short-lived product/ops capability: (1) a usage quarantine flow that isolates suspect usage from final invoicing, and (2) an automated tenant-credit simulator (sandbox) that lets CS/Finance/PMs preview prorations, provisional credits, and final invoice outcomes before accepting/committing ledger changes. Goal: reduce mean time-to-resolution for contested usage, lower manual adjustment errors, and provide reproducible experiments for pricing/packaging A/Bs that touch credits/proration logic.

title: Python SDK: output contracts, stream chunking ergonomics, and retry guidance
preview: Deliver a cohesive Python SDK surface for typed response contracts (dataclasses first with pydantic interop), streaming chunk ergonomics, structured-output helpers, and clear retry semantics/examples. This ticket covers API shape, helper utilities, example recipes, and a compatibility shim to migrate existing projects that use raw dict responses.
text: Deliver a cohesive Python SDK surface for typed response contracts (dataclasses first with pydantic interop), streaming chunk ergonomics, structured-output helpers, and clear retry semantics/examples. This ticket covers API shape, helper utilities, example recipes, and a compatibility shim to migrate existing projects that use raw dict responses.

title: Investigate and remediate int8 kernel-selection + KV-cache alignment causing silent accuracy drift and intermittent crash loops
preview: Summary: Following the rollout of the quant-autotuner-v2 release to production GPU pools, we observed two correlated failure modes for a subset of models on int8 execution paths: (1) silent accuracy degradation on long-context summarization and reranking workloads (quality regression without obvious error logs) and (2) intermittent crash loops on nodes running a fused-attention kernel when sequence lengths hit 2048+ under burst load. This ticket tracks the investigation, mitigations, and action items to prevent recurrence.
text: Summary: Following the rollout of the quant-autotuner-v2 release to production GPU pools, we observed two correlated failure modes for a subset of models on int8 execution paths: (1) silent accuracy degradation on long-context summarization and reranking workloads (quality regression without obvious error logs) and (2) intermittent crash loops on nodes running a fused-attention kernel when sequence lengths hit 2048+ under burst load. This ticket tracks the investigation, mitigations, and action items to prevent recurrence.
